title: Venmo Prestige Social Series
summary: Driving generational relevance through edutainment. We developed the strategy and launch of Venmo's first long-form social series starring Rachel Sennott and Jordan Firstman.
body: Venmo's product is everywhere, but its core users are aging out and the fight for Gen Z attention has turned into a knife fight, with every fintech and neobank competing for the same friend groups and the same loyalty. We worked directly with Venmo's marketing leadership and social teams to develop an entertainment-first creative strategy that mirrored the way friends actually play, behave, and pay together, then turned the brand into a series people would want to watch.

Bringing on Rachel Sennott and Jordan Firstman, two zillennial actors at the center of the cultural conversation, moved the work from advertising into prestige social comedy. The three-part series rolled out across social, OOH, streaming, podcasts, and audio, shot on 35mm by Anora cinematographer Drew Daniels, directed by Stink Films' Ramez Silyan, with an original score from Mura Masa. Sennott and Firstman were in the writers' room, shaped the wardrobe, and had creative input on the final cuts, so the chemistry on screen reads as the real friendship it is.

body.text: We would be Venmoing each other and telling each other, 'Get the sushi.' So that made it really fun to shoot.

body: The product story lives inside the comedy rather than around it. Venmo Cashback and Venmo Groups become the texture of how these characters move through their day, splitting tabs, paying back roommates, building little economies of friendship. The result is generational relevance earned through edutainment: a brand that doesn't sell to Gen Z so much as join their feed, which is exactly the bar the next generation of fintech has to clear.
Allbriton Robbins - Creative Strategy Director, TikTok Creative Lab
Venmo - Marketing leadership and social teams
